volumeTitle: Casina. The Casket Comedy. Curculio. Epidicus. The Two Menaechmuses
label: Curculio (Section 1.2.132b - Section 1.3.198)

sections.label: Section 1.2.132b
sections.original: Noli , taceo . écce autem bibit árcus , pluet credo hércle hodie .
sections.translation: I ’ ll hold my tongue ; ( the old woman drinks ) but see , the rainbow ’ s drinking [*] ; I ’ faith , I do believe it will rain to-day .

sections.label: Section 1.2.133
sections.original: Iamne égo huic dico ?
sections.translation: Am I to say then to her —?

sections.label: Section 1.2.133b
sections.original: Quid díces ?
sections.translation: Say what ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.133c
sections.original: Me periísse .
sections.translation: That I ’ m quite undone .

sections.label: Section 1.2.133d
sections.original: Age dice .
sections.translation: Well then , say so .

sections.label: Section 1.2.133e
sections.original: Anus , aúdi .
sections.translation: Old lady , do listen . I wish you to know this ; to my sorrow , I ’ m quite undone .

sections.label: Section 1.2.135
sections.original: At pól ego oppidó servata .
sections.translation: But , i ’ faith , for my part , I ’ m altogether brought to life . But why is it that you are pleased to say you are quite undone ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.137b
sections.original: Quia id quod amo cáreo .
sections.translation: Because I ’ m deprived of the object which I love . ( Pretends to weep )

sections.label: Section 1.2.138
sections.original: Phaedrome mi , ne plora amabo .
sections.translation: My dear Phaedromus , prithee , do not weep ; do you take care that I ’ m not thirsty , I ’ ll at once bring out here for you the object which you love . ( Goes into the house .)

sections.label: Section 1.2.140
sections.original: Tibine égo , si fidem servás mecum , vineám pro aurea statuá statuam ,
sections.translation: Assuredly , if you keep faith with me , in place of a golden statue , I ’ ll erect for you one of wine [*] , which shall be a memorial of your gullet Palinurus , who on earth will be so blest as myself , if she comes to me ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.142b
sections.original: Edepol qui amát , si eget , misera ádficitur , ere , aerúmna .
sections.translation: By my faith , he who is in love , if he is in want as well , is afflicted with a dreadful malady .

sections.label: Section 1.2.143
sections.original: Non íta res est , nam cónfido parasítum hodie adventúrum
sections.translation: Such is not the case with me ; for I feel sure that this very day my Parasite will come hither to me with the money .

sections.label: Section 1.2.144b
sections.original: Magnum ínceptas , si id éxpectas quod núsquamst .
sections.translation: You attempt something mighty , if you expect that which nowhere exists .

sections.label: Section 1.2.145
sections.original: Quid si ádeam ad fores atque óccentem ?
sections.translation: What if I approach the door , and trill a carol [*] ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.145b
sections.original: Si lúbet , neque veto neque iúbeo ,
sections.translation: If you choose ; I neither bid nor request you , since , my master , I see that you are of manners and disposition thus changed .

sections.label: Section 1.2.147
sections.original: Péssuli , heus péssuli , vós salutó lubens ,
sections.translation: Bolts , O ye bolts , with pleasure do I salute you . I love you , I court you , I seek you , and you entreat ; most kindly lend your aid to me in love ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.150
sections.original: fite causa mea ludii barbari ,
sections.translation: become , for my sake , as though play-actors [*] from foreign climes ; leap upwards pray , and send out of doors this fair one , who drains my blood for me distractedly in love . ( Addressing PALINURUS .) Look at that , how those most accursed bolts sleep on , and none the quicker for my sake do they bestir themselves .

sections.label: Section 1.2.155
sections.original: re spicio , nihili meam vos gratiam facere .
sections.translation: I see quite clearly that you don ’ t value my esteem at all . Hist ! hush , hush !

sections.label: Section 1.2.156b
sections.original: Taceo hercle equidem .
sections.translation: I ’ troth , for my part I ’ m silent enough .

sections.label: Section 1.2.156c
sections.original: Sentio sonitum .
sections.translation: I hear a noise ; at last , i ’ faith , these bolts have become complaisant to me .

sections.label: Section 1.3.158
sections.original: Plácide egredere et sónitum prohibe fórium et crepitum cárdinum ,
sections.translation: Come softly out , and prevent the noise of the doors and the creaking of the hinges , my dear Planesium , that our master mayn ’ t perceive that that is going on which we are doing here .

sections.label: Section 1.3.160
sections.original: mane , suffundam aquolam .
sections.translation: Stay , I ’ ll pour a little water [*] on it .

sections.label: Section 1.3.160b
sections.original: Viden ut anus tremula medicinam facit ?
sections.translation: Do you see how the palsied hag is giving her dose ? She herself has learnt right well to drink up the wine ; to the door she ’ s giving water for it to drink .

sections.label: Section 1.3.162
sections.original: Vbi tu es , qui me convadatu'sconvadatus es Veneriis vadimoniis ?
sections.translation: Where are you — you who have summoned me on the recognizances of Venus [*] ? To you do I present myself , and , in the same way do I call on you , on the other hand , to present yourself to me .

sections.label: Section 1.3.164
sections.original: Assum ; nam si absim , haud recusem quin mihi male sit , mel meum .
sections.translation: Here I am ; for could I be absent , I wouldn ’ t repine for any misfortune to befall me , my honey .

sections.label: Section 1.3.165
sections.original: Anime mi , me procul amantem abesse haud consentaneumst .
sections.translation: My life , it is not becoming for one thus in love to be at a distance .

sections.label: Section 1.3.166
sections.original: Palinure , Palinure .
sections.translation: Palinurus , Palinurus !

sections.label: Section 1.3.166b
sections.original: Eloquere , quid est quod Palinurum voces ?
sections.translation: Say on ; why is it that you call upon Palinurus ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.167
sections.original: Est lepida .
sections.translation: She is a charming one .

sections.label: Section 1.3.167b
sections.original: Nimis lepida .
sections.translation: Aye , too charming .

sections.label: Section 1.3.167c
sections.original: Sum deus .
sections.translation: I am a God .

sections.label: Section 1.3.167d
sections.original: Immo homo haud magni preti .
sections.translation: Why , no — a mortal , of no great value .

sections.label: Section 1.3.168
sections.original: Quid vidisti aut quid videbis magis dis aequiparabile ?
sections.translation: What have you seen , or what will you see , more nearly to be compared with the Gods ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.169
sections.original: Male valere te , quod mi aegrest .
sections.translation: You are not in your senses , master ; a thing that ’ s grievous to me .

sections.label: Section 1.3.169b
sections.original: Male mi morigeru'smorigerus es , tace .
sections.translation: You are not sufficiently respectful to me : hold your tongue .

sections.label: Section 1.3.170
sections.original: Ipsus se excruciat qui homo quod amat videt nec potitur dum licet .
sections.translation: The person that sees [*] the object which he loves , and enjoys not the opportunity while he may , is one who torments his own self .

sections.label: Section 1.3.171
sections.original: Recte obiurgat . sane haud quicquamst , magis quod cupiam iam diu .
sections.translation: Rightly does he rebuke me ; really there ’ s nothing which for this long time past I have more eagerly desired .

sections.label: Section 1.3.172
sections.original: Tene me , amplectere ergo .
sections.translation: Clasp me , embrace me then .

sections.label: Section 1.3.172b
sections.original: Hoc etiam est quam ob rem cupiam vivere .
sections.translation: This , too , is a reason for which I could wish to live ; because your master restrains , you , in secret do I court you .

sections.label: Section 1.3.173b
sections.original: Prohibet ? nec prohibere quit ,
sections.translation: Restrain me ? He neither can restrain me , nor will he restrain me , unless death should separate my soul from you .

sections.label: Section 1.3.175
sections.original: Enim vero nequeo durare quin ego erum accusem meum :
sections.translation: Of a truth , I cannot refrain from giving a lecture to my master ; for , really , it is good to love in a moderate degree ; to distraction , it is not good ; but to love to entire distraction , is the thing that my master ’ s doing .

sections.label: Section 1.3.178
sections.original: Sibi sua habeant regna reges , sibi divitias divites ,
sections.translation: Let monarchs keep their kingdoms to themselves , the rich their riches to themselves , to themselves their honors , to themselves their prowess , to themselves their combats , to themselves their battles ;

sections.label: Section 1.3.180
sections.original: dum mi abstineant invidere , sibi quisque habeant quod suom est .
sections.translation: so long as they abstain from envying me , let each one of them keep what is his own .

sections.label: Section 1.3.181
sections.original: Quid tu ? Venerin pervigilare te vovisti , Phaedrome ?
sections.translation: What say you , sir ? Have you made a vow , Phaedromus , you ’ d watch the night through for Venus ? For really , upon my faith , before very long hence the dawn will be breaking .

sections.label: Section 1.3.182b
sections.original: Tace .
sections.translation: Do hold your tongue .

sections.label: Section 1.3.183
sections.original: Quid , taceam ? quin tu is dormitum ?
sections.translation: Why hold my tongue ? What , are you going to sleep ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.183b
sections.original: Dormio , ne occlamites .
sections.translation: I am asleep ; don ’ t you make a noise .

sections.label: Section 1.3.184
sections.original: Tu quidem vígilas .
sections.translation: Why , but you ’ re broad awake .

sections.label: Section 1.3.184b
sections.original: At meo more dormio : hic somnust mihi .
sections.translation: Aye , but after my own fashion I ’ m asleep ; this is my slumber .

sections.label: Section 1.3.185
sections.original: Heus tu , mulier , male mereri de inmerente inscitia est .
sections.translation: A word with you , madam ; ’ tis thoughtlessness to treat amiss one who deserves it not .

sections.label: Section 1.3.186
sections.original: Irascere , si te edentem hic a cibo abigat .
sections.translation: You would be angry , if , when you are eating , he were to drive you away [*] from your food .

sections.label: Section 1.3.186b
sections.original: Ilicet ,
sections.translation: It ’ s all over with him . I see that these two are equally in love to distraction ; and both of them are mad . D ’ ye see how intensely they hug each other ? They cannot embrace enough . ( Addressing them .) Are you going to part yet ?

sections.label: Line 189b
sections.original: Nullum homini est perpetuom bonum :
sections.translation: No human being has blessings that last [*] for ever . To this pleasure , then , is that plague added .

sections.label: Section 1.3.190b
sections.original: Quid ais , propudium ?
sections.translation: What say you , you shocking hussy [*] ? What , you little tipsy ninny [*] , are even you with your owlish eyes [*] to be calling me a plague , you whipper-snapper ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.192b
sections.original: Tun meam Venerem vituperas ?
sections.translation: What , you abusing my own Venus ? And really , is a slave well trounced with the rod to be commencing a discussion with myself ? But , by the powers , you ’ ve surely said that to your own misfortune .

sections.label: Section 1.3.195
sections.original: em tibi male dictis pro istis , dictis moderari ut queas .
sections.translation: There , take that , by way of punishment for this abusive language , that you may be able to put a check upon your speech .

sections.label: Section 1.3.196
sections.original: Tuam fidem , Venus noctuvigila .
sections.translation: Your aid , I pray , you night-watching Venus .

sections.label: Section 1.3.196b
sections.original: Pergin etiam , verbero ?
sections.translation: What , do you still persist , whip-scoundrel ? ( Strikes hint .)

sections.label: Section 1.3.197
sections.original: Noli , amabo , verberare lapidem , ne perdas manum .
sections.translation: Don ’ t , there ’ s a dear , be beating a stone , lest you should hurt your hand .

sections.label: Section 1.3.198
sections.original: Flagitium probrumque magnum , Phaedrome , expergefacis :
sections.translation: You perpetrate , Phaedromus , a flagitious and a shameful deed of great enormity ; one who directs you aright , you pummel with your fists ; her you are in love with , a mere nonentity .
